title: Pizza margherita
subtitle: Tomato, mozzarella, and basil on a quick same-day dough
excerpt: The benchmark pizza: San Marzano-style tomato sauce, torn mozzarella, and fresh basil on a thin, fast-rise dough baked at the maximum heat your oven can manage. This same-day version uses slightly more yeast for a 2- to 4-hour room-temperature rise instead of an overnight cold ferment.
sourceNotes: The margherita is attributed by tradition to Naples in 1889, when pizzaiolo Raffaele Esposito reportedly made a pizza for Queen Margherita of Savoy in the colours of the Italian flag. The story is likely apocryphal but the combination (tomato, mozzarella, basil) predates the naming. Documented in Neapolitan pizza literature from the early twentieth century.

content.text: Pizza margherita is the control recipe for every pizza maker. If you can make this well, everything else follows. The same-day dough uses more yeast than a cold-ferment and rises at room temperature in two to four hours, enough time for some flavour development, not enough for the complexity of a 48-hour cold rise. For a Friday night, start the dough when you get home.

The hotter the oven, the better the pizza. Set your oven to its maximum. If you have a pizza steel or stone, preheat it for at least 45 minutes. If you do not, a heavy baking tray preheated upside-down works as a reasonable substitute. The basil goes on after baking, not before.

items.name: Mozzarella
items.prepNote: torn and dried on kitchen paper

content.text: Dissolve the {{yeast-fast-action}} of yeast in the {{water}} of lukewarm water. Add the {{00-flour}} of flour, {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt, and {{olive-oil}} of olive oil and mix until a rough dough forms. Knead for 8 to 10 minutes until smooth and elastic. Divide into 4 balls, place in a lightly oiled bowl, cover, and rest at room temperature for 2 to 4 hours until doubled in size.

Place a pizza stone or heavy baking tray on the top shelf of the oven. Heat to maximum, 250°C or higher if your oven allows. Allow 45 minutes for the stone to get fully hot. Drain the {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tinned tomatoes well and crush by hand. Season with salt.

On a well-floured surface, press each dough ball out with your fingertips to about 28 cm in diameter, leaving a thicker border at the edge. Spread a thin layer of tomato sauce over the base. Scatter over a quarter of the {{mozzarella}} mozzarella. Drizzle with {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} of olive oil. Slide onto the hot stone and bake for 8 to 12 minutes until the base is crisp and the edges are golden with some dark spots. Scatter {{basil}} basil leaves over the top and serve immediately.

items.fix: The water should feel warm but not hot, around 35 to 40°C; check the yeast expiry date
items.cause: Water too hot (kills the yeast) or too cold (slows it), or yeast past its date
items.symptom: Dough has not risen after 2 hours

items.fix: Use less sauce than feels natural, a thin smear, not a layer; dry the mozzarella on kitchen paper for 10 minutes; preheat the stone for longer
items.cause: Too much sauce, or mozzarella not dried, or oven not hot enough
items.symptom: Base is soggy in the centre

items.fix: Use semolina on the peel rather than flour for a better sliding surface; once the pizza is topped, move it to the oven within 2 minutes
items.cause: Not enough flour on the peel, or waited too long after topping the pizza
items.symptom: Pizza sticks to the peel when sliding into the oven

content.text: Whether or not the margherita was really made for a queen in 1889, the combination of tomato, mozzarella, and basil was already well established in Neapolitan pizza culture by then. The dish became the default reference for a good pizza because it has nowhere to hide: there are three main elements and each one must be right. Most domestic pizza problems are oven-temperature problems. A hotter oven fixes most of them.
